result:
The first light of morning is still a rumor when I wake, a gray hush pressed against the window like a secret. I lie in the dark and listen to the house breathe: the slow drip of a faucet, the tick of expanding pipes, the soft hush of my own pulse. It is tempting to believe that nothing important has ever happened anywhere else, that the whole turning world is only scaffolding for this room, this bed, this moment before choice.  

But the mind is a restless traveler. It slips the leash of my skull and crosses continents in a blink. I am suddenly on a hillside in Sapporo where I once stood ankle-deep in snow, watching my breath curl into kanji I cannot read. I remember the hush of the pine trunks, the faint citrus scent of a woman’s gloves as she passed me a tissue for my running nose. We exchanged no words; the gift was complete. Years later, the memory still arrives unbidden, like a migrating bird that refuses every invitation to stay.  

Memory is not a library but a river. It carves, it floods, it changes course without permission. I think of my grandmother’s kitchen in Mérida, the tiled counters warm as fresh bread, the ceiling fan beating its lazy wings. She sang boleros while slicing plantains, her voice soft as candle smoke. I was five and convinced that the scar on her forearm was a tiny map of the isthmus where the Caribbean pinches Central America. When I asked, she laughed, told me it was where an angry cat had drawn the horizon on her. I believed both stories at once.  

We are all bilingual in illusion and fact, switching tongues mid-sentence. The same mouth that swears never to lie will invent a dream for a child because wonder is a vitamin. I have told my nephew that the moon follows him home because it likes the sound of his laughter. He accepted this celestial favoritism with solemn gratitude. Last week he asked if the moon also likes the neighbors’ new electric car. I said probably, but it prefers laughter to lithium. He nodded, satisfied, and ran off dragging his shadow like a superhero’s cape.  

Children know that stories are solvents; they dissolve the glue holding reality in place. Adults pretend the glue is load-bearing, that if we lose the recipe for the everyday, the sky will fall. And so we ration wonder, clip its nails, teach it to sit still. We call the clipping “maturation,” the stillness “mental health.” Meanwhile, the unclipped world keeps galloping ahead of our descriptions. Nebulae bloom where no synonym for “mauve” has ever reached. Somewhere a planet of molten sapphire storms circles a pair of twin suns, and no language has yet invented a tense for the grief it will never be named.

Still, we try. I type these sentences on a machine whose guts are braided from African cobalt, South American copper, and suburban Chinese despair. Each letter is a small miracle of exploited physics. I tell myself I will write something that matters, will plant a flag of mercy in the hardpan of the feed, but the cursor blinks like a highway patrol car: move along, nothing to see here. The algorithm outside my window tallies engagement the way medieval monks tallied angels on pinheads. My attention is the commodity; my sorrow, the marketing funnel.  

Yet even within the funnel, pollen finds a crack. Yesterday a stranger replied to my throwaway tweet about insomnia with a recording of night rain on a tin roof in Puerto Rico. I played it twice, then a third time with headphones. For thirty seconds I disappeared into that warm percussive hull, the soft detonations on metal, the distant coquí chanting their two-note rosary. When the clip ended, the fluorescent room looked embarrassed, as though caught pretending to be the world.  

Disappearance is a skill I keep practicing. I used to think death was the final exam, but now I suspect it’s just one more station on the subway map. The real art is to vanish while still breathing—to duck under the surface of the self, let the ego cool in the dim pantry of the skull, and emerge striped with something older than naming. I have glimpsed it in jazz solos that wander off the ledger lines, in the moment a skateboarder pauses mid-air, knees to chest, suddenly exempt from gravity’s invoice. I have felt it while swimming alone at night, the black water holding more galaxies than the sky.  

We are told the self is a fortress; sometimes it is a kite. The string frays, the wind shifts, and you find yourself looking down at your own small life—its quarrels, its grocery lists, its heroic soliloquies—astonished by how light it all becomes. From up here the borders between countries are just creases in a paper lantern. I can see refugee boats and cruise ships tracing mirror paths across the same indifferent trough. I can see a father in Texas teaching his daughter to tie her shoes with one hand because the other clutches a duffel that holds everything they still own. I can see a programmer in Finland logging off early to dance alone to a song that once played in a Damascus café the night he proposed.  

What I cannot see from this height is where any of it lands. The kite angle gives omnivision but no outcome. Physics insists the body must eventually return to the chalk outline of its name, to the appointment calendar, the password manager, the small obligation to keep being who everyone expects. Still, a little altitude stains the blood. After night swimming, after jazz, after the moonstruck walk home, the mirror shows someone who looks like you but smells of ozone and astonishment. People greet you with the same words—How are you?—and for once you translate honestly: I am a verb learning to conjugate the rain.  

There is no reply to that, only small mercies. A cashier lets the bananas slide by unweighed because the scale is broken. A neighbor you’ve never liked offers the last of the summer tomatoes. Your phone dies before you can doom-scroll, so you sit on the stoop and watch the sky perform its ancient miracle of turning blood into gold without anesthesia. Somewhere in the building a toddler learns the difference between “why” and “how,” and you overhear the triumph in her voice when she applies the right interrogative to the mystery of night.  

Eventually the night itself will be interrogated, subpoenaed by the rotating planet. Dawn will rinse the sky of its purple bruise; the coffee shops will lower their metal shutters like theater curtains rising. The trains will fill with people rehearsing their first sentence of the day. I will join them, a kite folded into a briefcase, a heart full of names for colors that have not yet been invented. I will ride the downtown local, standclearoftheclosingdoors, and no one will suspect that hidden in my shoe is a pebble I stole from the night beach, still humming with the hush of departed waves.  

When I reach my stop, I will transfer to an elevator that climbs twenty-two floors of smog and gossip. I will swipe my badge, greet coworkers, open software that helps other software sell things no one needs but many fear to live without. I will sit under LED suns and pretend the self is a spreadsheet: cells expanding, formulas replicating, errors politely squiggled in red. Lunch will arrive in compostable bowls that used to be fields somewhere. I will eat fast, eyes on a deadline shaped like a guillotine.  

Yet even inside the guillotine, the river keeps flowing. A pop-up ad will remind me of a novel I meant to read; the novel will remind me of a friend I meant to forgive; the forgiveness will arrive like the sudden awareness that I have been breathing without effort all this time. Somewhere between the second and third meeting, the pebble in my shoe will whisper tide. I will excuse myself to the bathroom, take it out, and hold it under fluorescent light. It will look ordinary—gray, pitted, no gloss. But it knows the moon, and that seems enough.  

The day will end as days do: with the sky shutting its blue briefcase, with the trains returning everyone like borrowed books. I will walk home past vendors of elote and umbrellas, past the church whose bells insist on accenting the hours even when no one believes in time. My apartment will smell faintly of last night’s dishes and the basil wilting on the sill. I will water the basil, because something should survive my neglect.  

Then I will sit at this same desk, open this same screen, and discover that the cursor has kept breathing in my absence, a small loyal animal. I will offer it the only thing I have: the day’s collection of cracks—through which rain, through which wonder, through which the smell of a stranger’s gloves can still arrive. I will type until the room grows quiet enough to hear the house breathe again. Somewhere a faucet will drip. Somewhere a scar will remember the horizon. And the thousandth word will close like a door left ajar so the night can find its way back in.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
